text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the seminal vesicles, dissecting out the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The vas deferens is cauterized and divided, while the seminal vesicle is freed by dividing its lateral attachments. A plane is created between the seminal vesicles and prostate anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly by sharply dividing the Denonvilliers fascia. The seminal vesicles and vas deferens on the opposite side are similarly dissected and divided.

A 3-0 PDS suture on a straight needle is passed through the anterior abdominal wall to elevate the bladder and seminal vesicles anteriorly and superiorly, preventing the bladder from obstructing the dissection field. The surgeon continues dissection between the seminal vesicle and prostate anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly, carefully dividing the Denonvilliers fascia while avoiding entry into the perirectal fat or rectum.

The surgeon dissects the lateral prostatic pedicles, clips them with hemologs, and divides them to maintain a blood-free surgical field. The pedicle on the opposite side is similarly clipped and divided.

The surgeon dissects the seminal vesicle and prostate away from the bladder, gently teasing the bladder upwards to reveal the prostate. The posterior bladder wall is separated from the posterior surface of the prostate. Dissection continues on both sides of the prostate, moving from posterior to lateral, and the posterior prostato-vesical junction is divided, allowing the prostate to be delivered into the surgical field.

The Foley catheter is visible following the division of the posterior prostato-vesical junction. Both lobes of the prostate are exposed as dissection continues laterally and anteriorly. Cold scissors and electrocautery are used to divide the prostate-vesical junction, and the prostate is dissected laterally, being carefully teased off the levator muscles.

The surgeon places a stay suture on the posterior bladder wall before dividing the anterior attachments between the bladder and prostate. The anterior prostatocycle junction is divided, freeing the prostate from the bladder. Dissection proceeds to the prostatic apex, where the urethra is carefully divided to ensure adequate length for urethrovicycle anastomosis. The specimen is dissected all around and delivered.

The surgeon begins the urethrovicycle anastomosis at the twelve o'clock position using a 30 cm 3-0 V-lock suture. The suture is placed continuously from the twelve o'clock to the six o'clock position. The Foley catheter is visible, aiding in identifying the urethral lumen and ensuring accurate needle placement.

The surgeon completes the erythrocycal anastomosis by using a V-lock suture from twelve o'clock to six o'clock position on the opposite side. The two sutures meet at six o'clock and are tied together to ensure a watertight seal. Bleeding points are sutured to achieve hemostasis, finalizing the anastomosis.
